Module 1 PASSIVE HOUSE INTRODUCTION Version 1.2 -- 1 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 2 COURSE ROADMAP Module 1 Passive House Introduction Module 2 Insulated Envelope Module 3 Airtightness Module 4 Thermal Bridging Module 5 Passive House Windows Module 6 Mechanical Ventilation Module 7 Heating and Domestic Hot Water Module 8 Cooling Module 9 Passive House Certification Module 11 Passive House Quality Assurance Module 12 Bidding Module 13 designPH Module 14 Passive House Planning Package Module 15 CPHD Exam Overview Module 10 Economics -- 2 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 3 LEARNING OBJECTIVES • Understanding of the climate-independent Passive House definition and its derivation: • "A Passive House is a building, for which thermal comfort (ISO 7730) can be achieved solely by post- heating or post-cooling of the fresh air mass, which is required to achieve sufficient indoor air quality conditions – without the need for additional recirculation of air". • Understanding of the certification criteria for Passive House buildings and retrofits using Passive House components (EnerPHit). • Knowledge of the key parameters (e.g. in the Verification worksheet of the PHPP) heating load, cooling load, annual heating demand, annual cooling and dehumidification demand, n50 value, primary energy (non-renewable and renewable PER), final energy, energy services and frequency of overheating. • Definition and influence of the reference areas and volumes used in Passive House design and certification. • Basic understanding of the issue of assessing sustainability of the energy demand of buildings in the context of a changing energy supply system. Energy balancing • Basic principles of energy balancing: balance boundary, balance envelope, balance equations. • Contributions to heat loss: transmission if applicable, ventilation/infiltration, cooling if applicable. • Contributions to heat gains: transmission if applicable, internal heat gains, passive solar gains, heating if applicable. • Sustainability evaluation in the context of the overall energy supply system (primary energy non- renewable/primary energy renewable PER). -- 3 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 4 1960’S ‘PASSIVE’ SOLAR HOMES -- 4 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 5 THE SASKATCHEWAN CONSERVATION HOUSE (1977) • R-40 walls • R-60 ceiling • Triple-glazed windows • Heat-recovery ventilation • Drain water heat exchanger • 0.8 ac/h @ 50 pascals The Saskatchewan Conservation House, 1977 Source: Harold Orr, The Saskatchewan Conservation House Source: Harold Orr, The Saskatchewan Conservation House In 1995, Rob Dumont summed up the overall lessons from the Saskatchewan Conservation House: 1. Simple is better than complicated 2. Passive is better than active 3. Moving parts fail -- 5 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 6 DARMSTADT PASSIVE HOUSE (1991) Four private clients formed the ‘Developers Society Passive House‘ and commissioned the architects Bott/Ridder/Westermeyer with the planning of a row of houses with four flats, each with 1679 ft² of living space. The building was provided with a precise data measurement acquisition system to examine the achievement of the objectives. -- 6 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 7 Performance Standard • Airtight Construction • Super Insulation • Triple Pane Windows • ERV / HRV ventilation • Low Yearly Energy Demand • Low Peak Power Loads • Thermal Bridge Free • PHPP = Quality Design Performance Standard • Airtight Construction • Super Insulation • Triple Pane Windows • ERV / HRV ventilation • Low Yearly Energy Demand • Low Peak Power Loads • Thermal Bridge Free • PHPP = Quality Design WHAT IS A ‘PASSIVE HOUSE’? -- 7 of 77 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training Module 1 | Passive House Introduction 8 PASSIVE HOUSE MYTHS • It’s only applicable to ‘houses’ • You can’t open the windows • Air quality in an airtight building must be awful • Too restrictive on aesthetics • It’s too expensive $$$ ALL FALSE -- 8 of 77 --

Module 4 THERMAL BRIDGING Version 1.2 -- 1 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 2 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging COURSE ROADMAP Module 1 Passive House Introduction Module 2 Insulated Envelope Module 3 Airtightness Module 4 Thermal Bridging Module 5 Passive House Windows Module 6 Mechanical Ventilation Module 7 Heating and Domestic Hot Water Module 8 Cooling Module 9 Passive House Certification Module 11 Passive House Quality Assurance Module 12 Bidding Module 13 designPH Module 14 Passive House Planning Package Module 15 CPHD Exam Overview Module 10 Economics -- 2 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 3 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging LEARNING OBJECTIVES • Acquaintance with thermal bridge coefficients (exterior and interior dimensions) and qualitative analyses of building envelopes with regard to potential thermal bridges. • Distinction between linear and point thermal bridges. • Understanding of the principle of thermal bridge free design. Approach to be used for cases where a completely thermal bridge free solution is not feasible. • Quantitative estimation of simple thermal bridges. • Solutions for typical thermal bridge structural connections. • Calculation of transmission heat losses arising from thermal bridges. • Introduction to modelling thermal bridges in PHPP. -- 3 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 4 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging M4.1 PRINCIPLES OF THERMAL BRIDGING -- 4 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 5 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging WHAT IS A THERMAL BRIDGE? Part of the building envelope where the otherwise uniform thermal resistance is significantly reduced by: full or partial penetration of the insulating layers by materials with a different thermal conductivity and/or a change in thickness of the insulating layers and/or a difference between internal and external areas, such as occurs at wall/floor/ceiling junctions. -- 5 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 6 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging TWO TYPES OF THERMAL BRIDGES Linear thermal bridges • Found at connections between different planes of the envelope (example, wall to roof) • The magnitude of a linear thermal bridge is determined by its ‘Psi Value’, denoted by Ψe, measured in Btu/hr.ft.ºF Point thermal bridges • Found at point penetrations of the envelope (example, wall ties through insulation layer) • The magnitude of a point thermal bridge is determined by its ‘Chi Value’, denoted by , typically reported in Btu/hr.ºF -- 6 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 7 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging THERMAL ‘SHORTCUTS’ THROUGH THE ENVELOPE -- 7 of 58 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 8 Module 4 | Thermal Bridging THERMAL BRDGES ARE EVERYWHERE! -- 8 of 58 --

Energy balancing & install details Insulated Envelope – Moisture & construction types Section 6 -- 3 of 121 -- Certified Passive House Designer/Consultant Training 4 Section 3-1 | Mechanical Ventilation LEARNING OBJECTIVES (1) Basic principles of Passive House ventilation Why is ventilation essential? • Understanding of the requirements for hygienic air, fresh air quantity that is necessary per person, extract air demand, minimum air change rate. • Understanding of the relationship between relative indoor air humidity and effective air exchange. • Knowledge of the most important indoor air contaminants. Knowledge of the CO2 criterion. Resultant fresh air volume flows for hygienically adequate ventilation. • Relationship between relative indoor air humidity and sources of humidity inside the building, fresh air supply rate, and external temperature. Risks relating to high air change rates at low external temperatures. What can be done if this cannot be avoided for some important reason? Natural and ‘Purge’ ventilation • Driving forces of natural ventilation (qualitative understanding). • Types of natural ventilation: joints and cracks, tilted windows, intensive ventilation through windows. • Influences on natural ventilation: typical air change rates (qualitative understanding). • Why is natural ventilation unsuitable for Passive Houses in regions with significant temperature differences between the inside and outside? (Unreliableness, heat loss). Balanced supply and extract air system with heat recovery • Zoning in a floor plan: supply air zone, air transfer zone, extract air zone. Common Passive House Myths include: It's only applicable to houses. You can't open windows. Air quality in an airtight building must be awful. It's too aesthetically restrictive. It's too expensive. All of the above  Correct Correct 6/27/25, 9:14 PM Introduction M1 & M1.1 Quiz - The Passive House Network https://passivehousenetwork.org/courses/certified-passive-house-designer-course-on-demand/lessons/introduction-m1-m1-1-quiz/quizzes/introduction-… 2/4 -- 2 of 4 -- Passive House is based on achieving fundamental goals through calculated energy performance, providing great freedom of design and use, for a variety of building types, uses, and styles. In Passive House, we focus first on designing a great thermal enclosure that establishes low energy demand, comfort and health. True  Correct False Correct Think of it like a thermos or climate-specific clothing. On a cold winter day dressed with a good parka, pants, hat, mittens and so on, you can stay quite warm and comfortable. Typical building construction is more like just throwing on a hoodie and requires lots more energy to compensate for its poor performance. The basic principles of Passive House include 5 parts. Pick them: Continuous enclosure insulation  Correct Enclosure Airtightness  Correct Thermal Bridge Free Enclosure  Correct Contemporary architectural design Correct 6/27/25, 9:14 PM Introduction M1 & M1.1 Quiz - The Passive House Network https://passivehousenetwork.org/courses/certified-passive-house-designer-course-on-demand/lessons/introduction-m1-m1-1-quiz/quizzes/introduction-… 3/4 -- 3 of 4 -- High-Performance Windows & Doors  Mechanical Ventilation with Heat Recovery  Correct Correct Appropriate insulation levels, window performance, and ventilation heat recovery efficiency will be based on specific climate conditions, each becoming more rigorous as climates become more extreme. The airtightness goal is different and remains uniform in all climates, as does the definition of thermal bridge-free construction. Active heating and cooling are frowned upon, and their use is a failure of a project’s design. True False  Correct Correct Due to extreme weather conditions such as heat waves and cold snaps, it is often impossible to eliminate active heating and cooling, even in relatively “easy” climates. So, the goal is to optimize the building and minimize the size of the active systems. The systems can be run on electricity or fossil fuels, but electrification is encouraged, and we see heat pumps in wide use to provide combined heating and cooling. 6/27/25, 9:14 PM Introduction M1 & M1.1 Quiz - The Passive House Network https://passivehousenetwork.org/courses/certified-passive-house-designer-course-on-demand/lessons/introduction-m1-m1-1-quiz/quizzes/introduction-… 4/4 -- 4 of 4 --

In Passive House energy calculations match the terms to definitions: Sort elements Energy Demand is the total amount of energy used over the course of a year, indicated as Q or q  Correct Energy Load is the peak amount of energy needed for the coldest night and hottest day, indicated as P or p  Correct Correct The capital Q & P are used for the whole building totals, while lowercase q and p are used for per-square-foot calculations. 6/27/25, 9:13 PM Introduction M1.2 Quiz - The Passive House Network https://passivehousenetwork.org/courses/certified-passive-house-designer-course-on-demand/lessons/introduction-m1-2-quiz/quizzes/m1-introduction-… 2/8 -- 2 of 8 -- If you have a 2,000 SF building (and we know the maximum heat demand “q” allowed by Passive House is 4.75kBtu/SF*Year), what is the total amount of heating energy allowed for use over the course of the year? 9,500 Btu/year 9,500 kBtu/year  Correct 421 Btu/SF*year 421 KBtu/SF Correct The heat demand maximum of 4.75 is expressed in kilo Btus per square foot per year, so we can simply multiply the total floor area by 4.75 to get the answer in kBtu/SF*yr. Note carefully all the units, as the right units are essential for the right calculations. The units will be tricky at first, but with practice, they will become familiar. Peak Load (or Specific Load) “p” is also a per square foot calculation that describes the maximum capacity to heat or cool over an hour, so the units are Btu/(hr*SF).
